31: 32: --- 33: 34: ## Overview 35: 36: MemoryTree gives AI-assisted repositories a durable memory layer that survives across sessions, clients, branches, and machines. 37: 38: This repository ships two things: 39: 40: 1. A reusable skill defined by [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) 41: 2. A Node.js CLI for workspace setup, transcript import, session recall, report generation, heartbeat automation, and managed local hosting 42: 43: The current implementation is built around three storage scopes: 44: 45: - The development repository you actively edit 46: - A dedicated MemoryTree worktree that the heartbeat can update safely 47: - A global archive under `~/.memorytree/` for cross-project transcript storage and indexing 48: 49: That model lets MemoryTree keep your main development flow clean while still importing transcripts, generating reports, and optionally publishing Memory-only updates from an isolated branch. 50: 51: ## Quick Start 52: 53: ### Install as a skill 54: 55: Claude Code: 56: 57: ```bash 58: git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow 59: cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow 60: npm install 61: npm run build 62: ``` 63: 64: Codex: 65: 66: ```bash 67: git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow 68: cd ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow 69: npm install 70: npm run build 71: ``` 72: 73: Windows PowerShell (Codex): 74: 75: ```powershell 76: git clone https://github.com/beyondchenlin/memorytree-workflow.git ` 77: $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\skills\memorytree-workflow 78: 79: cd $env:USERPROFILE\.codex\skills\memorytree-workflow 80: npm install 81: npm run build 82: ``` 83: 84: If your installer expects a skill path, use the repository root. `SKILL.md` lives at the top level. 85: 86: Optional: expose the CLI as `memorytree` in your shell: 87: 88: ```bash 89: npm link 90: ``` 91: 92: ### Use the skill 93: 94: In Claude Code, run: 95: 96: ```text 97: /memorytree-workflow 98: ``` 99: 100: The skill detects whether the current repository is `not-installed`, `partial`, or `installed`, then takes the next appropriate step immediately. 101: 102: ### Use the CLI directly 103: 104: `memorytree init` and `memorytree upgrade` only create or update repository files. They do not register the repository with heartbeat or modify `~/.memorytree/config.toml` by themselves. 105: 106: Recommended first-time setup for a repository that should use heartbeat: 107: 108: ```bash 109: memorytree upgrade --root . --format json 110: memorytree daemon quick-start --root . 111: memorytree caddy enable --root . 112: ``` 113: 114: Useful day-to-day commands: 115: 116: ```bash 117: memorytree recall --root . 118: memorytree discover --root . --client all --scope current-project --format json 119: memorytree report build --root . --no-ai --locale en 120: memorytree caddy status --root . 121: memorytree daemon run-once --root . --force 122: ``` 123: 124: ## Architecture 125: 126: MemoryTree now runs as a worktree-aware system, not just a single-folder transcript importer: 127: 128: ```text 129: Development repository 130: AGENTS.md 131: Memory/01_goals ... Memory/05_archive 132: | 133: | sync active context into 134: v 135: Dedicated MemoryTree worktree 136: isolated memorytree branch 137: Memory/06_transcripts 138: Memory/07_reports 139: | 140: | mirror matching transcripts, commit/push Memory-only changes, 141: | build report output 142: v 143: Global archive ~/.memorytree/ 144: config.toml 145: alerts.json 146: worktrees/ 147: transcripts/raw 148: transcripts/clean 149: transcripts/index 150: caddy/ 151: ``` 152: 153: The heartbeat copies active project context from the development directory into the dedicated worktree, processes transcript imports there, and then syncs transcript/report outputs back into the development directory. 154: 155: ### Per-repository layout 156: 157: After initialization or upgrade, a repository can contain: 158: 159: ```text 160: AGENTS.md 161: Memory/ 162: 01_goals/ 163: 02_todos/ 164: 03_chat_logs/ 165: 04_knowledge/ 166: 05_archive/ 167: 06_transcripts/ 168: clean/ 169: manifests/ 170: raw/ 171: 07_reports/ 172: ``` 173: 174: ### Global layout 175: 176: Shared state lives under `~/.memorytree/`: 177: 178: ```text 179: ~/.memorytree/ 180: config.toml 181: alerts.json 182: heartbeat.lock 183: logs/ 184: worktrees/ 185: caddy/ 186: Caddyfile 187: sites/ 188: transcripts/ 189: raw/ 190: clean/ 191: index/ 192: manifests/ 193: sessions.jsonl 194: search.sqlite 195: ``` 196: 197: ## Capabilities 198: 199: | Capability | Current behavior | 200: |---|---| 201: | Workspace scaffolding | `init` and `upgrade` create `Memory/` plus `AGENTS.md` without overwriting stronger existing repo policy | 202: | Transcript archive | `discover` scans default client stores, while `import` handles explicit transcript files and mirrors matching sessions into the repo | 203: | Session recall | `recall` runs an on-demand sync and returns the latest prior session for the current repository across clients | 204: | Worktree-backed heartbeat | `daemon register` and `daemon quick-start` create an isolated MemoryTree worktree, schedule runs, and keep context/output in sync | 205: | Report generation | `report build` creates a multi-page static site with transcripts, goals, todos, knowledge, archive, projects, graph, search, tags, and RSS | 206: | Managed local hosting | `caddy enable|disable|status` writes project-scoped Caddy fragments for long-running local report hosting | 207: | Git safety | Automatic repo-local commits stay confined to MemoryTree branches and skip raw transcript mirrors until raw uploads are approved | 208: 209: ## Transcript Sources 210: 211: Default discovery currently scans these client stores: 212: 213: | Client | Source patterns | 214: |---|---| 215: | Codex | `~/.codex/sessions/**/*.jsonl` | 216: | Claude Code | `~/.claude/projects/**/*.jsonl` | 217: | Gemini CLI | `~/.gemini/tmp/*/checkpoints/**/*.json`, `~/.gemini/tmp/*/checkpoints/**/*.jsonl`, `~/.gemini/history/**/*.json`, `~/.gemini/history/**/*.jsonl`, `~/.gemini/chats/**/*.json`, `~/.gemini/chats/**/*.jsonl` | 218: 219: Explicit one-off import also supports transcript files that are not part of default discovery, including `doubao` exports: 220: 221: ```bash 222: memorytree import --source /path/to/doubao_export.txt --client doubao --root . --force-repo 223: ``` 224: 225: Import rules: 226: 227: - Raw transcripts stay unchanged as evidence. 228: - Clean transcripts are generated deterministically by code. 229: - Repo-local mirrors are limited to transcripts that belong to the current repository unless `--force-repo` is used deliberately. 230: - Unrelated transcripts are archived in the global archive only. 231: - Raw transcript mirrors are excluded from automatic staging until the user approves raw uploads for that repository. 232: 233: ## Session Continuity 234: 235: When you ask for the most recent prior conversation, MemoryTree can: 236: 237: 1. scan the supported local transcript stores immediately 238: 2. import any new matching transcripts 239: 3. query the global index for the current project 240: 4. exclude the current session by activation time 241: 5. return the latest prior clean transcript content and metadata 242: 243: This works across clients, so a project session recorded in one assistant can be recalled from another. 244: 245: ## Report Site 246: 247: `memorytree report build` generates a static HTML site under `Memory/07_reports/`. 248: 249: Current report coverage includes: 250: 251: - dashboard 252: - transcript list and per-session pages 253: - goals, todos, knowledge, archive, and projects pages 254: - search, filters, and snippets 255: - graph view and backlink navigation 256: - tags and cached AI summaries 257: - breadcrumbs and table of contents 258: - RSS feed 259: - optional GitHub Pages deployment 260: - optional webhook notification 261: - English and Simplified Chinese locales 262: 263: Build it manually: 264: 265: ```bash 266: memorytree report build --root . --no-ai --locale en --report-base-url https://memory.example.com 267: ``` 268: 269: Temporary fallback preview: 270: 271: ```bash 272: memorytree report serve --dir ./Memory/07_reports --port 10010 273: ``` 274: 275: `report serve` is intended for temporary preview. For long-running local access, keep Caddy pointed at `Memory/07_reports/`. 276: 277: ## Heartbeat 278: 279: The heartbeat is the background automation layer. It is now project-aware and worktree-aware. 280: 281: Recommended setup: 282: 283: ```bash 284: memorytree daemon quick-start --root . 285: ``` 286: 287: Use this as the quick-install path when you want the current repository to join heartbeat with the recommended defaults. 288: 289: If you want more control, install the machine scheduler once and then register each repository with your own branch, interval, and report settings: 290: 291: ```bash 292: memorytree daemon install --interval 5m --auto-push true 293: memorytree daemon register --root . --branch memorytree-docs --heartbeat-interval 10m --refresh-interval 30m --auto-push false --generate-report true --report-port 10010 294: memorytree daemon run-once --root . --force 295: ``` 296: 297: Current execution flow: 298: 299: ```text 300: Acquire lock 301: -> load config 302: -> select due projects 303: -> ensure dedicated MemoryTree worktree 304: -> sync AGENTS.md + active Memory context into the worktree 305: -> discover and import matching transcripts 306: -> commit/push Memory-only changes on the MemoryTree branch when allowed 307: -> build Memory/07_reports when report generation is enabled and the run imported new transcripts 308: -> sync transcripts and reports back to the development directory 309: -> release lock 310: ``` 311: 312: Important branch-safety rules: 313: 314: - Repo-local transcript mirroring and automatic commits only happen on a dedicated MemoryTree branch. 315: - On non-MemoryTree branches, the heartbeat still imports into the global archive but keeps the repository clean. 316: - If no Git remote exists, push is skipped and an alert is written. 317: - Push failures alert and retry once. 318: - Sensitive transcript patterns are logged as warnings only; they are not auto-deleted or auto-redacted. 319: 320: ## Caddy 321: 322: MemoryTree includes first-stage project-managed Caddy support for local report hosting. 323: 324: Recommended commands: 325: 326: ```bash 327: memorytree caddy enable --root . 328: memorytree caddy status --root . 329: memorytree caddy disable --root . 330: ``` 331: 332: What it manages: 333: 334: - `~/.memorytree/caddy/Caddyfile` 335: - `~/.memorytree/caddy/sites/.caddy` 336: - per-project port and exposure settings taken from `~/.memorytree/config.toml` 337: 338: Exposure modes: 339: 340: - `local`: bind to `127.0.0.1` and `localhost` only 341: - `lan`: bind on the configured port and expose LAN URLs for the machine's IPv4 interfaces 342: 343: For long-running local access, `caddy enable` is the primary path. `report serve` remains the lightweight fallback. 344: 345: ## Configuration 346: 347: Global settings live in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`. 348: 349: Example: 350: 351: ```toml 352: heartbeat_interval = "5m" 353: auto_push = true 354: log_level = "info" 355: generate_report = true 356: locale = "en" 357: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages" 358: cname = "memory.example.com" 359: webhook_url = "" 360: report_base_url = "https://memory.example.com" 361: report_port = 10010 362: report_exposure = "local" 363: 364: [[projects]] 365: id = "my-repo" 366: path = "/path/to/repo" 367: name = "my-repo" 368: development_path = "/path/to/repo" 369: memory_path = "/path/to/.memorytree/worktrees/my-repo" 370: memory_branch = "memorytree" 371: heartbeat_interval = "5m" 372: refresh_interval = "30m" 373: auto_push = true 374: generate_report = true 375: locale = "en" 376: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages" 377: report_base_url = "https://memory.example.com" 378: report_port = 10010 379: report_exposure = "local" 380: ``` 381: 382: Fields that matter most: 383: 384: | Field | Meaning | 385: |---|---| 386: | `development_path` | The directory you actively work in | 387: | `memory_path` | The dedicated MemoryTree worktree used by the heartbeat | 388: | `memory_branch` | The branch reserved for MemoryTree automation | 389: | `heartbeat_interval` | Per-project heartbeat cadence | 390: | `refresh_interval` | How often transcript/report outputs sync back to the development directory | 391: | `generate_report` | Whether heartbeat should build `Memory/07_reports` after transcript imports | 392: | `gh_pages_branch` | Branch used for report publishing when configured | 393: | `report_base_url` | Base URL for RSS and OG metadata | 394: | `report_port` | Default local report port for this project | 395: | `report_exposure` | `local` or `lan` exposure for managed Caddy hosting | 396: 397: `path` is still preserved for compatibility, but the worktree-aware flow is driven by `development_path` and `memory_path`. 398: 399: ## Git Safety 400: 401: MemoryTree is strict about not taking over your normal development flow. 402: 403: | Rule | Behavior | 404: |---|---| 405: | Branch isolation | Automatic repo-local transcript commits happen only on dedicated MemoryTree branches | 406: | Worktree isolation | Heartbeat automation can run from a dedicated worktree instead of your main working directory | 407: | Scope isolation | Repo mirrors include only the current project's transcripts unless you force a repo-local import | 408: | Raw upload control | Raw transcript files are not auto-staged until explicitly approved | 409: | Push safety | If no remote exists, push is skipped and an alert is written | 410: | Failure handling | Push failures alert and retry once; report and webhook failures do not abort the heartbeat cycle | 411: | Secret scanning | Transcript imports log potential secrets but do not auto-delete or auto-redact them | 412: 413: ## CLI 414: 415: All commands are available through `node dist/cli.js ...`, or `memorytree ...` if you ran `npm link`. 416: 417: | Command | Description | 418: |---|---| 419: | `memorytree init` | Initialize MemoryTree files in a repository without registering heartbeat | 420: | `memorytree upgrade` | Upgrade repository files to MemoryTree without registering heartbeat | 421: | `memorytree import --source ` | Import one transcript into the repo mirror and global archive | 422: | `memorytree discover` | Scan local client stores and import matching transcripts | 423: | `memorytree locale` | Detect the effective repository locale | 424: | `memorytree recall` | Run on-demand sync and return the latest prior session | 425: | `memorytree report build` | Build the static HTML report site | 426: | `memorytree report serve` | Temporarily preview the generated report over HTTP | 427: | `memorytree caddy enable` | Write/update the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy | 428: | `memorytree caddy disable` | Remove the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy | 429: | `memorytree caddy status` | Show whether the current project is connected to MemoryTree-managed Caddy | 430: | `memorytree daemon install` | Register the machine-level heartbeat scheduler | 431: | `memorytree daemon quick-start` | Quick install: connect the repository to heartbeat with the recommended defaults | 432: | `memorytree daemon register` | Advanced heartbeat setup with custom per-project settings | 433: | `memorytree daemon uninstall` | Remove the scheduled heartbeat task | 434: | `memorytree daemon run-once` | Execute one heartbeat cycle immediately | 435: | `memorytree daemon watch` | Development-only continuous heartbeat loop | 436: | `memorytree daemon status` | Show scheduler registration and lock status | 437: 438: ## Development And Validation 439: 440: Install dependencies: 441: 442: ```bash 443: npm install 444: ``` 445: 446: Build: 447: 448: ```bash 449: npm run build 450: ``` 451: 452: Validate locally: 453: 454: ```bash 455: npm run typecheck 456: npm run lint 457: npm test 458: npm run test:e2e 459: ``` 460: 461: Current validation shape: 462: 463: - Node.js 20+ is required locally 464: - CI runs on Node.js 20, 22, and 24 across Linux, macOS, and Windows 465: - The repository includes focused unit coverage for transcript parsing/import, project upgrade, report rendering, heartbeat orchestration, and Caddy management 466: - The E2E workflow runs the CLI end-to-end on Node.js 20 across Linux, macOS, and Windows 467: 468: ## Reference Docs 469: 470: | File | Purpose | 471: |---|---| 472: | [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) | Skill entry point and behavioral contract | 473: | [docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md](docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md) | Chinese walkthrough of the skill layers and startup flow | 474: | [docs/璁板繂鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮涓ら樁娈垫敼閫犳柟妗?md](docs/璁板繂鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮涓ら樁娈垫敼閫犳柟妗?md) | Two-phase proposal for making `memorytree` the only shared memory source and turning the development directory into a local cache mirror | 475: | [docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md](docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md) | Worktree-based heartbeat design and rollout notes | 476: | [docs/caddy-auto-management-design.md](docs/caddy-auto-management-design.md) | Design notes for managed Caddy support | 477: | [references/project-detection.md](references/project-detection.md) | Install-state detection | 478: | [references/memory-layout.md](references/memory-layout.md) | Memory folder layout | 479: | [references/update-rules.md](references/update-rules.md) | Goal, todo, and chat-log update rules | 480: | [references/git-policy.md](references/git-policy.md) | Safe Git defaults and policy guidance | 481: | [references/heartbeat-scheduling.md](references/heartbeat-scheduling.md) | Heartbeat lifecycle and scheduler behavior | 482: | [references/global-configuration.md](references/global-configuration.md) | `~/.memorytree/` layout and config schema | 483: | [references/transcript-archive.md](references/transcript-archive.md) | Transcript import, cleaning, indexing, and recall | 484: | [references/locale-selection.md](references/locale-selection.md) | Locale detection rules | 485: | [references/response-language.md](references/response-language.md) | Reply-language precedence | 486: | [references/upgrade-path.md](references/upgrade-path.md) | Non-destructive adoption path | 487: 488: ## Update 489: 490: Claude Code: 491: 492: ```bash 493: cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow 494: git pull 495: npm install 496: npm run build 497: ``` 498: 499: Codex: 500: 501: ```bash 502: cd ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow 503: git pull 504: npm install 505: npm run build 506: ``` 507: 508: ## It is now project-aware and worktree-aware. 280: 281: Recommended setup: 282: 283: ```bash 284: memorytree daemon quick-start --root . 285: ``` 286: 287: Use this as the quick-install path when you want the current repository to join heartbeat with the recommended defaults. 288: 289: If you want more control, install the machine scheduler once and then register each repository with your own branch, interval, and report settings: 290: 291: ```bash 292: memorytree daemon install --interval 5m --auto-push true 293: memorytree daemon register --root . --branch memorytree-docs --heartbeat-interval 10m --refresh-interval 30m --auto-push false --generate-report true --report-port 10010 294: memorytree daemon run-once --root . --force 295: ``` 296: 297: Current execution flow: 298: 299: ```text 300: Acquire lock 301: -> load config 302: -> select due projects 303: -> ensure dedicated MemoryTree worktree 304: -> sync AGENTS.md + active Memory context into the worktree 305: -> discover and import matching transcripts 306: -> commit/push Memory-only changes on the MemoryTree branch when allowed 307: -> build Memory/07_reports when report generation is enabled and the run imported new transcripts 308: -> sync transcripts and reports back to the development directory 309: -> release lock 310: ``` 311: 312: Important branch-safety rules: 313: 314: - Repo-local transcript mirroring and automatic commits only happen on a dedicated MemoryTree branch. 315: - On non-MemoryTree branches, the heartbeat still imports into the global archive but keeps the repository clean. 316: - If no Git remote exists, push is skipped and an alert is written. 317: - Push failures alert and retry once. 318: - Sensitive transcript patterns are logged as warnings only; they are not auto-deleted or auto-redacted. 319: 320: ## Caddy 321: 322: MemoryTree includes first-stage project-managed Caddy support for local report hosting. 323: 324: Recommended commands: 325: 326: ```bash 327: memorytree caddy enable --root . 328: memorytree caddy status --root . 329: memorytree caddy disable --root . 330: ``` 331: 332: What it manages: 333: 334: - `~/.memorytree/caddy/Caddyfile` 335: - `~/.memorytree/caddy/sites/.caddy` 336: - per-project port and exposure settings taken from `~/.memorytree/config.toml` 337: 338: Exposure modes: 339: 340: - `local`: bind to `127.0.0.1` and `localhost` only 341: - `lan`: bind on the configured port and expose LAN URLs for the machine's IPv4 interfaces 342: 343: For long-running local access, `caddy enable` is the primary path. `report serve` remains the lightweight fallback. 344: 345: ## Configuration 346: 347: Global settings live in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`. 348: 349: Example: 350: 351: ```toml 352: heartbeat_interval = "5m" 353: auto_push = true 354: log_level = "info" 355: generate_report = true 356: locale = "en" 357: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages" 358: cname = "memory.example.com" 359: webhook_url = "" 360: report_base_url = "https://memory.example.com" 361: report_port = 10010 362: report_exposure = "local" 363: 364: [[projects]] 365: id = "my-repo" 366: path = "/path/to/repo" 367: name = "my-repo" 368: development_path = "/path/to/repo" 369: memory_path = "/path/to/.memorytree/worktrees/my-repo" 370: memory_branch = "memorytree" 371: heartbeat_interval = "5m" 372: refresh_interval = "30m" 373: auto_push = true 374: generate_report = true 375: locale = "en" 376: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages" 377: report_base_url = "https://memory.example.com" 378: report_port = 10010 379: report_exposure = "local" 380: ``` 381: 382: Fields that matter most: 383: 384: | Field | Meaning | 385: |---|---| 386: | `development_path` | The directory you actively work in | 387: | `memory_path` | The dedicated MemoryTree worktree used by the heartbeat | 388: | `memory_branch` | The branch reserved for MemoryTree automation | 389: | `heartbeat_interval` | Per-project heartbeat cadence | 390: | `refresh_interval` | How often transcript/report outputs sync back to the development directory | 391: | `generate_report` | Whether heartbeat should build `Memory/07_reports` after transcript imports | 392: | `gh_pages_branch` | Branch used for report publishing when configured | 393: | `report_base_url` | Base URL for RSS and OG metadata | 394: | `report_port` | Default local report port for this project | 395: | `report_exposure` | `local` or `lan` exposure for managed Caddy hosting | 396: 397: `path` is still preserved for compatibility, but the worktree-aware flow is driven by `development_path` and `memory_path`. 398: 399: ## Git Safety 400: 401: MemoryTree is strict about not taking over your normal development flow. 402: 403: | Rule | Behavior | 404: |---|---| 405: | Branch isolation | Automatic repo-local transcript commits happen only on dedicated MemoryTree branches | 406: | Worktree isolation | Heartbeat automation can run from a dedicated worktree instead of your main working directory | 407: | Scope isolation | Repo mirrors include only the current project's transcripts unless you force a repo-local import | 408: | Raw upload control | Raw transcript files are not auto-staged until explicitly approved | 409: | Push safety | If no remote exists, push is skipped and an alert is written | 410: | Failure handling | Push failures alert and retry once; report and webhook failures do not abort the heartbeat cycle | 411: | Secret scanning | Transcript imports log potential secrets but do not auto-delete or auto-redact them | 412: 413: ## CLI 414: 415: All commands are available through `node dist/cli.js ...`, or `memorytree ...` if you ran `npm link`. 416: 417: | Command | Description | 418: |---|---| 419: | `memorytree init` | Initialize MemoryTree files in a repository without registering heartbeat | 420: | `memorytree upgrade` | Upgrade repository files to MemoryTree without registering heartbeat | 421: | `memorytree import --source ` | Import one transcript into the repo mirror and global archive | 422: | `memorytree discover` | Scan local client stores and import matching transcripts | 423: | `memorytree locale` | Detect the effective repository locale | 424: | `memorytree recall` | Run on-demand sync and return the latest prior session | 425: | `memorytree report build` | Build the static HTML report site | 426: | `memorytree report serve` | Temporarily preview the generated report over HTTP | 427: | `memorytree caddy enable` | Write/update the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy | 428: | `memorytree caddy disable` | Remove the current project's managed Caddy fragment and reload Caddy | 429: | `memorytree caddy status` | Show whether the current project is connected to MemoryTree-managed Caddy | 430: | `memorytree daemon install` | Register the machine-level heartbeat scheduler | 431: | `memorytree daemon quick-start` | Quick install: connect the repository to heartbeat with the recommended defaults | 432: | `memorytree daemon register` | Advanced heartbeat setup with custom per-project settings | 433: | `memorytree daemon uninstall` | Remove the scheduled heartbeat task | 434: | `memorytree daemon run-once` | Execute one heartbeat cycle immediately | 435: | `memorytree daemon watch` | Development-only continuous heartbeat loop | 436: | `memorytree daemon status` | Show scheduler registration and lock status | 437: 438: ## Development And Validation 439: 440: Install dependencies: 441: 442: ```bash 443: npm install 444: ``` 445: 446: Build: 447: 448: ```bash 449: npm run build 450: ``` 451: 452: Validate locally: 453: 454: ```bash 455: npm run typecheck 456: npm run lint 457: npm test 458: npm run test:e2e 459: ``` 460: 461: Current validation shape: 462: 463: - Node.js 20+ is required locally 464: - CI runs on Node.js 20, 22, and 24 across Linux, macOS, and Windows 465: - The repository includes focused unit coverage for transcript parsing/import, project upgrade, report rendering, heartbeat orchestration, and Caddy management 466: - The E2E workflow runs the CLI end-to-end on Node.js 20 across Linux, macOS, and Windows 467: 468: ## Reference Docs 469: 470: | File | Purpose | 471: |---|---| 472: | [SKILL.md](SKILL.md) | Skill entry point and behavioral contract | 473: | [docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md](docs/memorytree-skill-features-and-startup.md) | Chinese walkthrough of the skill layers and startup flow | 474: | [docs/璁板繂鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮涓ら樁娈垫敼閫犳柟妗?md](docs/璁板繂鍒嗘敮鍗曚竴鐪熸簮涓ら樁娈垫敼閫犳柟妗?md) | Two-phase proposal for making `memorytree` the only shared memory source and turning the development directory into a local cache mirror | 475: | [docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md](docs/worktree-heartbeat-upgrade-plan.md) | Worktree-based heartbeat design and rollout notes | 476: | [docs/caddy-auto-management-design.md](docs/caddy-auto-management-design.md) | Design notes for managed Caddy support | 477: | [references/project-detection.md](references/project-detection.md) | Install-state detection | 478: | [references/memory-layout.md](references/memory-layout.md) | Memory folder layout | 479: | [references/update-rules.md](references/update-rules.md) | Goal, todo, and chat-log update rules | 480: | [references/git-policy.md](references/git-policy.md) | Safe Git defaults and policy guidance | 481: | [references/heartbeat-scheduling.md](references/heartbeat-scheduling.md) | Heartbeat lifecycle and scheduler behavior | 482: | [references/global-configuration.md](references/global-configuration.md) | `~/.memorytree/` layout and config schema | 483: | [references/transcript-archive.md](references/transcript-archive.md) | Transcript import, cleaning, indexing, and recall | 484: | [references/locale-selection.md](references/locale-selection.md) | Locale detection rules | 485: | [references/response-language.md](references/response-language.md) | Reply-language precedence | 486: | [references/upgrade-path.md](references/upgrade-path.md) | Non-destructive adoption path | 487: 488: ## Update 489: 490: Claude Code: 491: 492: ```bash 493: cd ~/.claude/skills/memorytree-workflow 494: git pull 495: npm install 496: npm run build 497: ``` 498: 499: Codex: 500: 501: ```bash 502: cd ~/.codex/skills/memorytree-workflow 503: git pull 504: npm install 505: npm run build 506: ``` 507: 508: ## Requirements 509: 510: - Node.js 20 or newer 511: - npm 512: - Git 513: - Claude Code, Codex, or another environment that can consume the skill or CLI 514: 515: ## License 516: 517: MIT

Using Node v20.20.1
Using Node v20.20.1
    1: # MemoryTree Worktree Heartbeat Upgrade Plan
    2: 
    3: ## Purpose
    4: 
    5: This document defines a worktree-based upgrade path for MemoryTree heartbeat.
    6: 
    7: The goal is to isolate automatic MemoryTree activity from the user's main development directory and main branch rules, while still making MemoryTree outputs visible inside the active development directory.
    8: 
    9: ## Problem Statement
   10: 
   11: The current behavior couples heartbeat too closely to the repository path that is registered in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
   12: 
   13: That creates two practical issues:
   14: 
   15: 1. If heartbeat runs in the main development directory, its automatic transcript imports and report outputs can interfere with the user's normal branch and commit flow.
   16: 2. If heartbeat does not run on a dedicated MemoryTree branch, repo-local transcript mirroring is skipped, which can leave the in-repo report looking empty even though data exists in the global archive.
   17: 
   18: ## Target Outcome
   19: 
   20: After this upgrade:
   21: 
   22: 1. Heartbeat runs only inside a dedicated Git worktree.
   23: 2. The main development directory does not run heartbeat.
   24: 3. The main development branch keeps its normal GitHub upload, review, and merge rules.
   25: 4. MemoryTree outputs generated in the worktree are copied back into the main development directory so the user can read and use them locally.
   26: 5. The system never relies on branch merges between the main development branch and the MemoryTree branch.
   27: 6. Project behavior is configured per project, while the operating system still runs a single global heartbeat task.
   28: 
   29: ## Terms
   30: 
   31: - `development directory`: the directory where the user is actively editing product code
   32: - `memory worktree`: a dedicated Git worktree used only for MemoryTree automation
   33: - `heartbeat`: the background process that discovers transcripts, imports them, and optionally builds reports
   34: - `directory sync`: copying selected files between the development directory and the memory worktree without merging branches
   35: 
   36: ## Config Scope
   37: 
   38: ### Current Limitation
   39: 
   40: The current implementation stores several behavior switches as global top-level fields in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
   41: 
   42: Examples:
   43: 
   44: - `heartbeat_interval`
   45: - `auto_push`
   46: - `generate_report`
   47: - `report_port`
   48: - `gh_pages_branch`
   49: - `report_base_url`
   50: 
   51: That means a quick-start action for one project can accidentally change behavior for another registered project.
   52: 
   53: ### Upgrade Direction
   54: 
   55: The target model is:
   56: 
   57: - one global scheduler
   58: - per-project execution settings
   59: 
   60: In other words:
   61: 
   62: - the operating system still runs one heartbeat task
   63: - each project entry carries its own behavior flags
   64: - the heartbeat loop decides project by project whether work is due and which options apply
   65: 
   66: ### Why A Single Global Scheduler
   67: 
   68: The upgrade should not create one OS scheduler task per project.
   69: 
   70: Reason:
   71: 
   72: - the current heartbeat runtime already assumes single-instance execution
   73: - one scheduler avoids lock contention and duplicate runs
   74: - one scheduler is simpler to operate across Windows, macOS, and Linux
   75: 
   76: Recommended model:
   77: 
   78: 1. install one global heartbeat task
   79: 2. run it on a short fixed cadence
   80: 3. evaluate each registered project independently
   81: 4. execute only the projects whose own interval and settings say they are due
   82: 
   83: ## Proposed Architecture
   84: 
   85: ### Development Directory
   86: 
   87: The development directory remains the user's normal working copy.
   88: 
   89: Responsibilities:
   90: 
   91: - product code editing
   92: - normal branch workflow
   93: - user-driven commits and pushes
   94: - reading MemoryTree outputs after they are copied back from the memory worktree
   95: 
   96: It must not run automatic heartbeat commits.
   97: 
   98: ### Memory Worktree
   99: 
  100: The memory worktree is a separate local folder created with `git worktree`.
  101: 
  102: Recommended characteristics:
  103: 
  104: - checked out on a dedicated branch, defaulting to `memorytree`
  105: - optionally allowed to use a custom branch name in detailed settings
  106: - registered as the project path used by heartbeat
  107: - allowed to run automatic MemoryTree writes, transcript imports, and report builds
  108: 
  109: Recommended path pattern:
  110: 
  111: - `~/.memorytree/worktrees/<repo-name>`
  112: 
  113: ## Why Worktree Instead Of Branch Merge
  114: 
  115: The intended model is not:
  116: 
  117: - run heartbeat on a side branch
  118: - merge that branch into the user's current development branch every 30 minutes
  119: 
  120: That approach would pollute branch history, create unnecessary merge commits, and mix MemoryTree automation with product-development Git flow.
  121: 
  122: The intended model is:
  123: 
  124: - keep heartbeat isolated in the memory worktree
  125: - copy selected MemoryTree files into the development directory
  126: - let the user decide how and when those files are committed on their active branch
  127: 
  128: This is directory-level synchronization, not branch-level merge automation.
  129: 
  130: ## Sync Model
  131: 
  132: ### Source Of Truth Rules
  133: 
  134: To avoid conflicts, each file group has only one source of truth.
  135: 
  136: #### Development Directory Is Source Of Truth
  137: 
  138: These files should be pushed from the development directory into the memory worktree before heartbeat runs:
  139: 
  140: - `AGENTS.md`
  141: - `Memory/01_goals/**`
  142: - `Memory/02_todos/**`
  143: - `Memory/03_chat_logs/**`
  144: - `Memory/04_knowledge/**`
  145: - `Memory/05_archive/**`
  146: 
  147: Reason:
  148: 
  149: These files reflect user-confirmed project context and active session state.
  150: 
  151: #### Memory Worktree Is Source Of Truth
  152: 
  153: These files should be copied from the memory worktree back into the development directory after heartbeat runs:
  154: 
  155: - `Memory/06_transcripts/**`
  156: - `Memory/07_reports/**`
  157: 
  158: Reason:
  159: 
  160: These files are the direct products of heartbeat-driven transcript import and report generation.
  161: 
  162: ### Visibility And Commit Semantics
  163: 
  164: When `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` are copied back into the development directory, they are expected to be visible to the user there.
  165: 
  166: `Memory/06_transcripts/**` is also expected to be committable from the development directory when the user decides that those files belong in the current branch.
  167: 
  168: This is an intentional product behavior, not a synchronization accident.
  169: 
  170: The only explicit exception remains any raw-transcript path that the repository policy keeps out of automatic staging until the user approves uploads.
  171: 
  172: ### Explicit Non-Goal
  173: 
  174: The same path must not be edited independently in both directories and then merged heuristically.
  175: 
  176: This upgrade should use directional copy rules, not two-way merge logic.
  177: 
  178: ## Sync Triggers
  179: 
  180: ### Background Sync
  181: 
  182: Recommended default behavior:
  183: 
  184: 1. One global heartbeat task runs on a short fixed interval.
  185: 2. Each project decides independently whether its own heartbeat interval has elapsed, for example `5m`.
  186: 3. A development-directory refresh runs on a slower per-project interval, for example `30m`.
  187: 4. That refresh copies the latest `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` from the memory worktree into the development directory.
  188: 
  189: This allows the user to see current MemoryTree data locally without running heartbeat in the development directory.
  190: 
  191: ### Manual Trigger
  192: 
  193: When the user manually triggers heartbeat from the directory they are currently editing, the system should perform the following sequence:
  194: 
  195: 1. Detect the current development directory.
  196: 2. Copy `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/01-05` from the development directory to the memory worktree.
  197: 3. Run heartbeat in the memory worktree.
  198: 4. Copy `Memory/06-07` from the memory worktree back to the current development directory.
  199: 
  200: This keeps the memory worktree aligned with the user's latest active context before running import and build operations.
  201: 
  202: ## Git Rules
  203: 
  204: ### Memory Worktree
  205: 
  206: The memory worktree may:
  207: 
  208: - run heartbeat
  209: - auto-import transcripts
  210: - auto-build reports
  211: - auto-commit MemoryTree-owned changes
  212: - auto-push its dedicated MemoryTree branch if enabled
  213: 
  214: ### Development Directory
  215: 
  216: The development directory must not:
  217: 
  218: - run automatic heartbeat commits
  219: - receive automatic branch merges from the memory worktree
  220: - have its normal branch protection or review rules bypassed
  221: 
  222: All GitHub upload behavior for the development directory remains under the user's existing branch logic.
  223: 
  224: Files copied back into the development directory may appear in `git status`, and that is expected.
  225: 
  226: ## Onboarding Flow
  227: 
  228: The intended activation flow should be:
  229: 
  230: 1. User enables MemoryTree for a repository.
  231: 2. System detects that the repository is not yet backed by a dedicated memory worktree.
  232: 3. System offers:
  233:    - `Quick Start`
  234:    - `Detailed Settings`
  235: 4. On confirmation, the system:
  236:    - creates or reuses a dedicated Git worktree
  237:    - checks out a dedicated MemoryTree branch inside that worktree
  238:    - registers the worktree path in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`
  239:    - preserves the user's development directory as the non-heartbeat working copy
  240: 
  241: ### Quick Start
  242: 
  243: Recommended defaults:
  244: 
  245: - global scheduler already installed or installed once during activation
  246: - project heartbeat interval: `5m`
  247: - project development-directory refresh interval: `30m`
  248: - project `auto_push = true`
  249: - project `generate_report = true`
  250: - project memory branch: `memorytree`
  251: - worktree-backed heartbeat enabled
  252: 
  253: ### Detailed Settings
  254: 
  255: The user may customize:
  256: 
  257: - project heartbeat interval
  258: - project development-directory refresh interval
  259: - project auto-push behavior
  260: - project report generation
  261: - project report publishing settings
  262: - dedicated memory branch name
  263: - raw transcript upload permission
  264: 
  265: ## Implementation Phases
  266: 
  267: ### Phase 1: Config Model
  268: 
  269: Add explicit configuration for:
  270: 
  271: - stable project identifier
  272: - development directory path
  273: - memory worktree path
  274: - project heartbeat interval
  275: - project development-directory refresh interval
  276: - project sync mode flags
  277: - project auto-push and report settings
  278: - per-project report publishing settings
  279: - last-run timestamps needed for due-check evaluation under one global scheduler
  280: 
  281: Example shape:
  282: 
  283: ```toml
  284: heartbeat_tick = "1m"
  285: 
  286: [[projects]]
  287: id = "memorytree-workflow"
  288: development_path = "D:/demo1/memorytree-workflow"
  289: memory_path = "C:/Users/ai/.memorytree/worktrees/memorytree-workflow"
  290: memory_branch = "memorytree"
  291: heartbeat_interval = "5m"
  292: refresh_interval = "30m"
  293: auto_push = true
  294: generate_report = true
  295: report_port = 10010
  296: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages"
  297: report_base_url = "https://example.github.io/memorytree-workflow"
  298: ```
  299: 
  300: ### Phase 2: Worktree Bootstrap
  301: 
  302: Add a bootstrap step that:
  303: 
  304: - verifies the repository is Git-backed
  305: - creates or reuses a dedicated worktree
  306: - ensures the worktree is on the project's configured MemoryTree branch, defaulting to `memorytree`
  307: 
  308: ### Phase 3: Directional Sync Engine
  309: 
  310: Add a file sync mechanism with whitelist-based copy rules:
  311: 
  312: - development directory to worktree for `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/01-05`
  313: - worktree to development directory for `Memory/06-07`
  314: 
  315: ### Phase 4: Manual Trigger Integration
  316: 
  317: Wire manual heartbeat execution so it always:
  318: 
  319: 1. syncs latest context into the worktree
  320: 2. runs heartbeat there
  321: 3. syncs outputs back to the invoking development directory
  322: 
  323: ### Phase 5: Background Refresh
  324: 
  325: Add a scheduled refresh path that copies worktree outputs back into the development directory on a slower cadence than heartbeat.
  326: 
  327: ## Current CLI Surface
  328: 
  329: The current implementation now exposes two concrete entry points for this design:
  330: 
  331: 1. `memorytree daemon register --root <repo> --quick-start`
  332: 2. `memorytree daemon register --root <repo> --heartbeat-interval <x> --refresh-interval <y> --auto-push <true|false> --generate-report <true|false> --branch <name>`
  333: 
  334: What `daemon register` now does:
  335: 
  336: - registers or updates the project in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`
  337: - writes both `development_path` and `memory_path`
  338: - creates or reuses the dedicated Git worktree
  339: - stores `memory_branch` per project
  340: - ensures the worktree is on the configured branch
  341: - uses `memorytree` for Quick Start by default, while detailed settings may override the branch name
  342: - auto-configures the first upstream binding when `auto_push` is enabled and a remote exists
  343: 
  344: The current implementation also extends manual execution:
  345: 
  346: - `memorytree daemon run-once --root <repo> --force`
  347: 
  348: That command targets the matching project directly, bypasses due checks, and runs the worktree-backed sync-and-heartbeat flow immediately.
  349: 
  350: ## Risks And Guardrails
  351: 
  352: ### Risk: Conflicting Edits
  353: 
  354: If both directories edit the same file family, the system can overwrite newer local content.
  355: 
  356: Guardrail:
  357: 
  358: - keep strict source-of-truth ownership by directory
  359: 
  360: ### Risk: Hidden Sync Failures
  361: 
  362: If copy steps fail silently, the user may believe heartbeat is working while the development directory stays stale.
  363: 
  364: Guardrail:
  365: 
  366: - surface sync status in logs and alerts
  367: 
  368: ### Risk: Wrong Registered Path
  369: 
  370: If heartbeat remains registered against the main development directory instead of the worktree, the isolation model breaks.
  371: 
  372: Guardrail:
  373: 
  374: - store and display both paths explicitly during activation and status checks
  375: 
  376: ### Risk: Cross-Project Identity Collisions
  377: 
  378: If project identity depends only on directory basename or slug matching, similarly named repositories can interfere with routing or transcript matching.
  379: 
  380: Guardrail:
  381: 
  382: - add a stable project identifier in project configuration
  383: - prefer exact configured paths over inferred names whenever possible
  384: 
  385: ## Current Recommendation
  386: 
  387: Use a dedicated memory worktree as the only heartbeat execution target.
  388: 
  389: Do not merge the MemoryTree branch into the user's active development branch on a timer.
  390: 
  391: Instead, copy MemoryTree output files from the worktree back into the development directory so the user can read them and decide how they participate in the normal Git workflow.
  392: 
  393: Keep the scheduler global, but make runtime behavior project-specific.

    1: # MemoryTree Worktree Heartbeat Upgrade Plan
    2: 
    3: ## Purpose
    4: 
    5: This document defines a worktree-based upgrade path for MemoryTree heartbeat.
    6: 
    7: The goal is to isolate automatic MemoryTree activity from the user's main development directory and main branch rules, while still making MemoryTree outputs visible inside the active development directory.
    8: 
    9: ## Problem Statement
   10: 
   11: The current behavior couples heartbeat too closely to the repository path that is registered in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
   12: 
   13: That creates two practical issues:
   14: 
   15: 1. If heartbeat runs in the main development directory, its automatic transcript imports and report outputs can interfere with the user's normal branch and commit flow.
   16: 2. If heartbeat does not run on a dedicated MemoryTree branch, repo-local transcript mirroring is skipped, which can leave the in-repo report looking empty even though data exists in the global archive.
   17: 
   18: ## Target Outcome
   19: 
   20: After this upgrade:
   21: 
   22: 1. Heartbeat runs only inside a dedicated Git worktree.
   23: 2. The main development directory does not run heartbeat.
   24: 3. The main development branch keeps its normal GitHub upload, review, and merge rules.
   25: 4. MemoryTree outputs generated in the worktree are copied back into the main development directory so the user can read and use them locally.
   26: 5. The system never relies on branch merges between the main development branch and the MemoryTree branch.
   27: 6. Project behavior is configured per project, while the operating system still runs a single global heartbeat task.
   28: 
   29: ## Terms
   30: 
   31: - `development directory`: the directory where the user is actively editing product code
   32: - `memory worktree`: a dedicated Git worktree used only for MemoryTree automation
   33: - `heartbeat`: the background process that discovers transcripts, imports them, and optionally builds reports
   34: - `directory sync`: copying selected files between the development directory and the memory worktree without merging branches
   35: 
   36: ## Config Scope
   37: 
   38: ### Current Limitation
   39: 
   40: The current implementation stores several behavior switches as global top-level fields in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`.
   41: 
   42: Examples:
   43: 
   44: - `heartbeat_interval`
   45: - `auto_push`
   46: - `generate_report`
   47: - `report_port`
   48: - `gh_pages_branch`
   49: - `report_base_url`
   50: 
   51: That means a quick-start action for one project can accidentally change behavior for another registered project.
   52: 
   53: ### Upgrade Direction
   54: 
   55: The target model is:
   56: 
   57: - one global scheduler
   58: - per-project execution settings
   59: 
   60: In other words:
   61: 
   62: - the operating system still runs one heartbeat task
   63: - each project entry carries its own behavior flags
   64: - the heartbeat loop decides project by project whether work is due and which options apply
   65: 
   66: ### Why A Single Global Scheduler
   67: 
   68: The upgrade should not create one OS scheduler task per project.
   69: 
   70: Reason:
   71: 
   72: - the current heartbeat runtime already assumes single-instance execution
   73: - one scheduler avoids lock contention and duplicate runs
   74: - one scheduler is simpler to operate across Windows, macOS, and Linux
   75: 
   76: Recommended model:
   77: 
   78: 1. install one global heartbeat task
   79: 2. run it on a short fixed cadence
   80: 3. evaluate each registered project independently
   81: 4. execute only the projects whose own interval and settings say they are due
   82: 
   83: ## Proposed Architecture
   84: 
   85: ### Development Directory
   86: 
   87: The development directory remains the user's normal working copy.
   88: 
   89: Responsibilities:
   90: 
   91: - product code editing
   92: - normal branch workflow
   93: - user-driven commits and pushes
   94: - reading MemoryTree outputs after they are copied back from the memory worktree
   95: 
   96: It must not run automatic heartbeat commits.
   97: 
   98: ### Memory Worktree
   99: 
  100: The memory worktree is a separate local folder created with `git worktree`.
  101: 
  102: Recommended characteristics:
  103: 
  104: - checked out on a dedicated branch, defaulting to `memorytree`
  105: - optionally allowed to use a custom branch name in detailed settings
  106: - registered as the project path used by heartbeat
  107: - allowed to run automatic MemoryTree writes, transcript imports, and report builds
  108: 
  109: Recommended path pattern:
  110: 
  111: - `~/.memorytree/worktrees/<repo-name>`
  112: 
  113: ## Why Worktree Instead Of Branch Merge
  114: 
  115: The intended model is not:
  116: 
  117: - run heartbeat on a side branch
  118: - merge that branch into the user's current development branch every 30 minutes
  119: 
  120: That approach would pollute branch history, create unnecessary merge commits, and mix MemoryTree automation with product-development Git flow.
  121: 
  122: The intended model is:
  123: 
  124: - keep heartbeat isolated in the memory worktree
  125: - copy selected MemoryTree files into the development directory
  126: - let the user decide how and when those files are committed on their active branch
  127: 
  128: This is directory-level synchronization, not branch-level merge automation.
  129: 
  130: ## Sync Model
  131: 
  132: ### Source Of Truth Rules
  133: 
  134: To avoid conflicts, each file group has only one source of truth.
  135: 
  136: #### Development Directory Is Source Of Truth
  137: 
  138: These files should be pushed from the development directory into the memory worktree before heartbeat runs:
  139: 
  140: - `AGENTS.md`
  141: - `Memory/01_goals/**`
  142: - `Memory/02_todos/**`
  143: - `Memory/03_chat_logs/**`
  144: - `Memory/04_knowledge/**`
  145: - `Memory/05_archive/**`
  146: 
  147: Reason:
  148: 
  149: These files reflect user-confirmed project context and active session state.
  150: 
  151: #### Memory Worktree Is Source Of Truth
  152: 
  153: These files should be copied from the memory worktree back into the development directory after heartbeat runs:
  154: 
  155: - `Memory/06_transcripts/**`
  156: - `Memory/07_reports/**`
  157: 
  158: Reason:
  159: 
  160: These files are the direct products of heartbeat-driven transcript import and report generation.
  161: 
  162: ### Visibility And Commit Semantics
  163: 
  164: When `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` are copied back into the development directory, they are expected to be visible to the user there.
  165: 
  166: `Memory/06_transcripts/**` is also expected to be committable from the development directory when the user decides that those files belong in the current branch.
  167: 
  168: This is an intentional product behavior, not a synchronization accident.
  169: 
  170: The only explicit exception remains any raw-transcript path that the repository policy keeps out of automatic staging until the user approves uploads.
  171: 
  172: ### Explicit Non-Goal
  173: 
  174: The same path must not be edited independently in both directories and then merged heuristically.
  175: 
  176: This upgrade should use directional copy rules, not two-way merge logic.
  177: 
  178: ## Sync Triggers
  179: 
  180: ### Background Sync
  181: 
  182: Recommended default behavior:
  183: 
  184: 1. One global heartbeat task runs on a short fixed interval.
  185: 2. Each project decides independently whether its own heartbeat interval has elapsed, for example `5m`.
  186: 3. A development-directory refresh runs on a slower per-project interval, for example `30m`.
  187: 4. That refresh copies the latest `Memory/06_transcripts/**` and `Memory/07_reports/**` from the memory worktree into the development directory.
  188: 
  189: This allows the user to see current MemoryTree data locally without running heartbeat in the development directory.
  190: 
  191: ### Manual Trigger
  192: 
  193: When the user manually triggers heartbeat from the directory they are currently editing, the system should perform the following sequence:
  194: 
  195: 1. Detect the current development directory.
  196: 2. Copy `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/01-05` from the development directory to the memory worktree.
  197: 3. Run heartbeat in the memory worktree.
  198: 4. Copy `Memory/06-07` from the memory worktree back to the current development directory.
  199: 
  200: This keeps the memory worktree aligned with the user's latest active context before running import and build operations.
  201: 
  202: ## Git Rules
  203: 
  204: ### Memory Worktree
  205: 
  206: The memory worktree may:
  207: 
  208: - run heartbeat
  209: - auto-import transcripts
  210: - auto-build reports
  211: - auto-commit MemoryTree-owned changes
  212: - auto-push its dedicated MemoryTree branch if enabled
  213: 
  214: ### Development Directory
  215: 
  216: The development directory must not:
  217: 
  218: - run automatic heartbeat commits
  219: - receive automatic branch merges from the memory worktree
  220: - have its normal branch protection or review rules bypassed
  221: 
  222: All GitHub upload behavior for the development directory remains under the user's existing branch logic.
  223: 
  224: Files copied back into the development directory may appear in `git status`, and that is expected.
  225: 
  226: ## Onboarding Flow
  227: 
  228: The intended activation flow should be:
  229: 
  230: 1. User enables MemoryTree for a repository.
  231: 2. System detects that the repository is not yet backed by a dedicated memory worktree.
  232: 3. System offers:
  233:    - `Quick Start`
  234:    - `Detailed Settings`
  235: 4. On confirmation, the system:
  236:    - creates or reuses a dedicated Git worktree
  237:    - checks out a dedicated MemoryTree branch inside that worktree
  238:    - registers the worktree path in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`
  239:    - preserves the user's development directory as the non-heartbeat working copy
  240: 
  241: ### Quick Start
  242: 
  243: Recommended defaults:
  244: 
  245: - global scheduler already installed or installed once during activation
  246: - project heartbeat interval: `5m`
  247: - project development-directory refresh interval: `30m`
  248: - project `auto_push = true`
  249: - project `generate_report = true`
  250: - project memory branch: `memorytree`
  251: - worktree-backed heartbeat enabled
  252: 
  253: ### Detailed Settings
  254: 
  255: The user may customize:
  256: 
  257: - project heartbeat interval
  258: - project development-directory refresh interval
  259: - project auto-push behavior
  260: - project report generation
  261: - project report publishing settings
  262: - dedicated memory branch name
  263: - raw transcript upload permission
  264: 
  265: ## Implementation Phases
  266: 
  267: ### Phase 1: Config Model
  268: 
  269: Add explicit configuration for:
  270: 
  271: - stable project identifier
  272: - development directory path
  273: - memory worktree path
  274: - project heartbeat interval
  275: - project development-directory refresh interval
  276: - project sync mode flags
  277: - project auto-push and report settings
  278: - per-project report publishing settings
  279: - last-run timestamps needed for due-check evaluation under one global scheduler
  280: 
  281: Example shape:
  282: 
  283: ```toml
  284: heartbeat_tick = "1m"
  285: 
  286: [[projects]]
  287: id = "memorytree-workflow"
  288: development_path = "D:/demo1/memorytree-workflow"
  289: memory_path = "C:/Users/ai/.memorytree/worktrees/memorytree-workflow"
  290: memory_branch = "memorytree"
  291: heartbeat_interval = "5m"
  292: refresh_interval = "30m"
  293: auto_push = true
  294: generate_report = true
  295: report_port = 10010
  296: gh_pages_branch = "gh-pages"
  297: report_base_url = "https://example.github.io/memorytree-workflow"
  298: ```
  299: 
  300: ### Phase 2: Worktree Bootstrap
  301: 
  302: Add a bootstrap step that:
  303: 
  304: - verifies the repository is Git-backed
  305: - creates or reuses a dedicated worktree
  306: - ensures the worktree is on the project's configured MemoryTree branch, defaulting to `memorytree`
  307: 
  308: ### Phase 3: Directional Sync Engine
  309: 
  310: Add a file sync mechanism with whitelist-based copy rules:
  311: 
  312: - development directory to worktree for `AGENTS.md` and `Memory/01-05`
  313: - worktree to development directory for `Memory/06-07`
  314: 
  315: ### Phase 4: Manual Trigger Integration
  316: 
  317: Wire manual heartbeat execution so it always:
  318: 
  319: 1. syncs latest context into the worktree
  320: 2. runs heartbeat there
  321: 3. syncs outputs back to the invoking development directory
  322: 
  323: ### Phase 5: Background Refresh
  324: 
  325: Add a scheduled refresh path that copies worktree outputs back into the development directory on a slower cadence than heartbeat.
  326: 
  327: ## Current CLI Surface
  328: 
  329: The current implementation now exposes two concrete entry points for this design:
  330: 
  331: 1. `memorytree daemon register --root <repo> --quick-start`
  332: 2. `memorytree daemon register --root <repo> --heartbeat-interval <x> --refresh-interval <y> --auto-push <true|false> --generate-report <true|false> --branch <name>`
  333: 
  334: What `daemon register` now does:
  335: 
  336: - registers or updates the project in `~/.memorytree/config.toml`
  337: - writes both `development_path` and `memory_path`
  338: - creates or reuses the dedicated Git worktree
  339: - stores `memory_branch` per project
  340: - ensures the worktree is on the configured branch
  341: - uses `memorytree` for Quick Start by default, while detailed settings may override the branch name
  342: - auto-configures the first upstream binding when `auto_push` is enabled and a remote exists
  343: 
  344: The current implementation also extends manual execution:
  345: 
  346: - `memorytree daemon run-once --root <repo> --force`
  347: 
  348: That command targets the matching project directly, bypasses due checks, and runs the worktree-backed sync-and-heartbeat flow immediately.
  349: 
  350: ## Risks And Guardrails
  351: 
  352: ### Risk: Conflicting Edits
  353: 
  354: If both directories edit the same file family, the system can overwrite newer local content.
  355: 
  356: Guardrail:
  357: 
  358: - keep strict source-of-truth ownership by directory
  359: 
  360: ### Risk: Hidden Sync Failures
  361: 
  362: If copy steps fail silently, the user may believe heartbeat is working while the development directory stays stale.
  363: 
  364: Guardrail:
  365: 
  366: - surface sync status in logs and alerts
  367: 
  368: ### Risk: Wrong Registered Path
  369: 
  370: If heartbeat remains registered against the main development directory instead of the worktree, the isolation model breaks.
  371: 
  372: Guardrail:
  373: 
  374: - store and display both paths explicitly during activation and status checks
  375: 
  376: ### Risk: Cross-Project Identity Collisions
  377: 
  378: If project identity depends only on directory basename or slug matching, similarly named repositories can interfere with routing or transcript matching.
  379: 
  380: Guardrail:
  381: 
  382: - add a stable project identifier in project configuration
  383: - prefer exact configured paths over inferred names whenever possible
  384: 
  385: ## Current Recommendation
  386: 
  387: Use a dedicated memory worktree as the only heartbeat execution target.
  388: 
  389: Do not merge the MemoryTree branch into the user's active development branch on a timer.
  390: 
  391: Instead, copy MemoryTree output files from the worktree back into the development directory so the user can read them and decide how they participate in the normal Git workflow.
  392: 
  393: Keep the scheduler global, but make runtime behavior project-specific.

  766: 
  767: function runCli(args: readonly string[], options: RunOptions = {}): CommandResult {
  768:   return runCommand(process.execPath, [cliPath, ...args], options)
  769: }
  770: 
  771: function runGit(args: readonly string[], cwd: string, env?: NodeJS.ProcessEnv): CommandResult {
  772:   return runCommand('git', args, { cwd, env })
  773: }
  774: 
  775: function runGitDir(args: readonly string[], gitDir: string): CommandResult {
  776:   return runCommand('git', ['--git-dir', gitDir, ...args], {})
  777: }
  778: 
  779: function runCommand(command: string, args: readonly string[], options: RunOptions): CommandResult {
  780:   const result = spawnSync(command, [...args], {
  781:     cwd: options.cwd,
  782:     env: {
  783:       ...process.env,
  784:       ...options.env,
  785:     },
  786:     encoding: 'utf-8',
  787:     stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'],
  788:     timeout: 120_000,
  789:   })
  790: 
  791:   if (result.error) {
  792:     throw result.error
  793:   }
  794: 
  795:   return {
  796:     status: result.status,
  797:     stdout: result.stdout ?? '',
  798:     stderr: result.stderr ?? '',
  799:   }
  800: }
  801: 
  802: async function startCli(
  803:   args: readonly string[],
  804:   options: RunOptions & { waitForUrl?: string } = {},
  805: ): Promise<RunningProcess> {
  806:   let stdout = ''
  807:   let stderr = ''
  808: 
  809:   const child = spawn(process.execPath, [cliPath, ...args], {
  810:     cwd: options.cwd,
  811:     env: {
  812:       ...process.env,
  813:       ...options.env,
  814:     },
  815:     stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe'],
  816:   })
  817: 
  818:   liveProcesses.push(child)
  819:   child.stdout.on('data', chunk => {
  820:     stdout += chunk.toString()
  821:   })
  822:   child.stderr.on('data', chunk => {
  823:     stderr += chunk.toString()
  824:   })
  825: 
  826:   await waitFor(async () => {
  827:     if (child.exitCode !== null) {
  828:       throw new Error([
  829:         `process exited early with code ${String(child.exitCode)}`,
  830:         'stdout:',
  831:         stdout.trim(),
  832:         'stderr:',
  833:         stderr.trim(),
  834:       ].join('\n'))
  835:     }
  836: 
  837:     if (!options.waitForUrl) {
  838:       return stdout.length > 0
  839:     }
  840: 
  841:     try {
  842:       const response = await fetch(options.waitForUrl)
  843:       return response.status === 200
  844:     } catch {
  845:       return false
  846:     }
  847:   }, 15_000)
  848: 
  849:   return {
  850:     child,
  851:     stdout: () => stdout,
  852:     stderr: () => stderr,
  853:   }
  854: }
  855: 
  856: function assertSuccess(result: CommandResult, label: string): void {
  857:   if (result.status !== 0) {
  858:     throw new Error([
  859:       `${label} failed with exit code ${String(result.status)}`,
  860:       'stdout:',
  861:       result.stdout.trim(),
  862:       'stderr:',
  863:       result.stderr.trim(),
  864:     ].join('\n'))
  865:   }
  866: }
  867: 
  868: function isolatedEnv(homePath: string): NodeJS.ProcessEnv {
  869:   return {
  870:     HOME: homePath,
  871:     USERPROFILE: homePath,
  872:   }
  873: }
  874: 
  875: function initGitRepo(root: string, branch: string): void {
  876:   mkdirSync(root, { recursive: true })
  877:   assertSuccess(runGit(['init', '-b', branch], root), 'git init')
  878:   assertSuccess(runGit(['config', 'user.email', 'e2e@example.com'], root), 'git config user.email')
  879:   assertSuccess(runGit(['config', 'user.name', 'MemoryTree E2E'], root), 'git config user.name')
  880: 
  881:   writeFileSync(join(root, 'README.md'), '# E2E Repo\n', 'utf-8')
  882:   assertSuccess(runGit(['add', 'README.md'], root), 'git add README')
  883:   assertSuccess(runGit(['commit', '-m', 'chore: init repo'], root), 'git commit init')
  884: }
  885: 
  886: function initBareRemote(root: string): void {
  887:   assertSuccess(
  888:     runCommand('git', ['init', '--bare', root], { cwd: sandboxRoot }),
  889:     'git init --bare',
  890:   )
  891: }
  892: 
  893: function commitAll(root: string, message: string): void {
  894:   assertSuccess(runGit(['add', '.'], root), 'git add .')
  895:   assertSuccess(runGit(['commit', '-m', message], root), `git commit ${message}`)
  896: }
  897: 
  898: function writeConfig(
  899:   homePath: string,
  900:   options: {
  901:     projectPath: string
  902:     autoPush: boolean
  903:     generateReport: boolean
  904:     ghPagesBranch?: string
  905:     cname?: string
  906:     webhookUrl?: string
  907:     reportBaseUrl?: string
  908:     reportPort?: number
  909:   },
  910: ): void {
  911:   const configDir = join(homePath, '.memorytree')
  912:   mkdirSync(configDir, { recursive: true })
  913: 
  914:   const configToml = [
  915:     'heartbeat_interval = "5m"',
  916:     `auto_push = ${options.autoPush ? 'true' : 'false'}`,
  917:     'log_level = "debug"',
  918:     `generate_report = ${options.generateReport ? 'true' : 'false'}`,

  936: 
  937: function writeCodexTranscript(
  938:   options: {
  939:     homeDir: string
  940:     repoPath: string
  941:     branch: string
  942:     stem: string
  943:     startedAt?: string
  944:     title?: string
  945:     userText?: string
  946:     assistantText?: string
  947:   },
  948: ): string {
  949:   const transcriptDir = join(options.homeDir, '.codex', 'sessions', 'e2e')
  950:   mkdirSync(transcriptDir, { recursive: true })
  951: 
  952:   const startedAt = options.startedAt ?? '2026-03-17T10:30:00Z'
  953:   const transcriptPath = join(transcriptDir, `${options.stem}.jsonl`)
  954:   const repoPath = toPosix(options.repoPath)
  955:   const records = [
  956:     {
  957:       type: 'session_meta',
  958:       payload: {
  959:         id: `sess-${options.stem}`,
  960:         title: options.title ?? `E2E ${options.stem}`,
  961:         cwd: repoPath,
  962:         git: { branch: options.branch },
  963:         timestamp: startedAt,
  964:       },
  965:     },
  966:     {
  967:       type: 'response_item',
  968:       timestamp: offsetIso(startedAt, 1),
  969:       payload: {
  970:         type: 'message',
  971:         role: 'user',
  972:         content: [{ type: 'input_text', text: options.userText ?? 'Capture this coding session.' }],
  973:       },
  974:     },
  975:     {
  976:       type: 'response_item',
  977:       timestamp: offsetIso(startedAt, 2),
  978:       payload: {
  979:         type: 'message',
  980:         role: 'assistant',
  981:         content: [{ type: 'text', text: options.assistantText ?? 'Captured and indexed.' }],
  982:       },
  983:     },
  984:     {
  985:       type: 'response_item',
  986:       timestamp: offsetIso(startedAt, 3),
  987:       payload: {
  988:         type: 'function_call',
  989:         name: 'read_file',
  990:         arguments: { path: 'README.md' },
  991:       },
  992:     },
  993:     {
  994:       type: 'response_item',
  995:       timestamp: offsetIso(startedAt, 4),
  996:       payload: {
  997:         type: 'function_call_output',
  998:         name: 'read_file',
  999:         output: '# E2E Repo',
 1000:       },
 1001:     },
 1002:   ]
 1003: 
 1004:   writeFileSync(
 1005:     transcriptPath,
 1006:     records.map(record => JSON.stringify(record)).join('\n') + '\n',
 1007:     'utf-8',
 1008:   )
 1009:   return transcriptPath
 1010: }
 1011: 
 1012: function listFiles(root: string): string[] {
 1013:   if (!existsSync(root)) return []
 1014: 
 1015:   const files: string[] = []
 1016:   const stack = [root]
 1017: 
 1018:   while (stack.length > 0) {
 1019:     const current = stack.pop()
 1020:     if (!current) continue
 1021: 
 1022:     for (const entry of readdirSync(current)) {
 1023:       const entryPath = join(current, entry)
 1024:       const stats = statSync(entryPath)
 1025:       if (stats.isDirectory()) {
 1026:         stack.push(entryPath)
 1027:       } else {
 1028:         files.push(toPosix(relative(root, entryPath)))
 1029:       }
 1030:     }
 1031:   }
 1032: 
 1033:   return files.sort()
 1034: }

 1036: async function getAvailablePort(): Promise<number> {
 1037:   return await new Promise<number>((resolvePort, reject) => {
 1038:     const server = createServer()
 1039:     server.unref()
 1040:     server.on('error', reject)
 1041:     server.listen(0, '127.0.0.1', () => {
 1042:       const address = server.address()
 1043:       if (address === null || typeof address === 'string') {
 1044:         server.close()
 1045:         reject(new Error('failed to allocate port'))
 1046:         return
 1047:       }
 1048: 
 1049:       server.close(error => {
 1050:         if (error) {
 1051:           reject(error)
 1052:           return
 1053:         }
 1054:         resolvePort(address.port)
 1055:       })
 1056:     })
 1057:   })
 1058: }

 1060: async function stopProcess(child: ChildProcessWithoutNullStreams): Promise<void> {
 1061:   if (child.exitCode !== null) {
 1062:     return
 1063:   }
 1064: 
 1065:   try {
 1066:     child.kill()
 1067:   } catch {
 1068:     return
 1069:   }
 1070: 
 1071:   await new Promise<void>(resolve => {
 1072:     const timeout = setTimeout(resolve, 2_000)
 1073:     const done = () => {
 1074:       clearTimeout(timeout)
 1075:       resolve()
 1076:     }
 1077:     child.once('exit', done)
 1078:     child.once('close', done)
 1079:   })
 1080: }
 1081: 
 1082: async function removeTreeWithRetry(root: string): Promise<void> {
 1083:   const deadline = Date.now() + 5_000
 1084: 
 1085:   // Windows can keep directory handles alive briefly after child.exit.
 1086:   let removed = false
 1087:   while (!removed) {
 1088:     try {
 1089:       rmSync(root, { recursive: true, force: true })
 1090:       removed = true
 1091:     } catch (error: unknown) {
 1092:       const err = error as NodeJS.ErrnoException
 1093:       if (!err.code || Date.now() >= deadline) {
 1094:         throw error
 1095:       }
 1096: 
 1097:       const code = String(err.code)
 1098:       if (code !== 'EBUSY' && code !== 'ENOTEMPTY' && code !== 'EPERM') {
 1099:         throw error
 1100:       }
 1101: 
 1102:       await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 100))
 1103:     }
 1104:   }
 1105: }
 1106: 
 1107: async function waitFor(
 1108:   predicate: () => boolean | Promise<boolean>,
 1109:   timeoutMs: number,
 1110:   intervalMs = 100,
 1111: ): Promise<void> {
 1112:   const deadline = Date.now() + timeoutMs
 1113:   let lastError: unknown = null
 1114: 
 1115:   while (Date.now() < deadline) {
 1116:     try {
 1117:       if (await predicate()) {
 1118:         return
 1119:       }
 1120:       lastError = null
 1121:     } catch (error) {
 1122:       lastError = error
 1123:     }
 1124: 
 1125:     await new Promise(resolveSleep => setTimeout(resolveSleep, intervalMs))
 1126:   }
 1127: 
 1128:   if (lastError instanceof Error) {
 1129:     throw lastError
 1130:   }
 1131:   if (lastError !== null) {
 1132:     throw new Error(String(lastError))
 1133:   }
 1134:   throw new Error('timed out waiting for condition')
 1135: }
 1136: 
 1137: function offsetIso(baseIso: string, seconds: number): string {
 1138:   return new Date(Date.parse(baseIso) + seconds * 1000).toISOString().replace(/\.\d{3}Z$/, 'Z')
 1139: }
 1140: 
 1141: function toPosix(value: string): string {
 1142:   return value.replace(/\\/g, '/')
 1143: }
 1144: 
 1145: function escapeToml(value: string): string {
 1146:   return value.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/"/g, '\\"')
 1147: }
